narrative: I'm sorry to share that our admissions model predicted a 54.7% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which tipped the decision against your application—law schools use this to prioritize students likely to succeed and pass the bar. Your profile shows strong points like an LSAT score of 38, which is above the typical student's 36.7, along with being a white male planning full-time studies, both common among applicants. However, your undergraduate GPA of 2.8 sits below the average 3.21, your Year 1 school decile is 1—putting you in the bottom tenth compared to the average fifth or sixth decile—and your Year 3 decile is similarly a 1, far below the typical 5.5. Middle family income is right around average, and your demographics align closely with most students in the dataset. When the model weighed these, your Year 3 decile had the strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction, followed closely by your Year 1 decile, then family income, LSAT, undergraduate GPA, racial background, full-time status, and gender—all nudging the prediction slightly toward failure rather than success. Compared to typical students who pass, your low deciles stand out as unusually weak signals of academic consistency in school, while even your solid LSAT didn't outweigh them enough. This ranking shows school performance in later years mattered most here, reflecting how the model prioritizes sustained grades for bar success. It's a tough outcome, but it highlights where your record diverges from stronger applicants who tend to cluster in higher deciles and GPAs. Looking ahead, you can't change past deciles, GPA, LSAT, income, or demographics, but retaking the LSAT for a higher score could help future applications if you aim above 38. More realistically for bar passage, focus on building study habits now that mimic top decile performers—consistent practice, time management, and seeking tutoring to boost your odds beyond what the model sees. Many students improve bar outcomes through dedicated prep courses or full-time focus without distractions. You're close at 54.7%; small shifts in preparation could flip that narrative for you. Keep pushing—you've got a good LSAT foundation to build on.
